title: The Macro Minute: Is consensus still wrong about tariffs?

Happy Wednesday out there team42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Wednesday,
September 10th, 2025. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. If you
would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk or to get on the right side
of market risk to the extent you haven't
been following KISS or Dr. Mo, you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So today's
key macro question is, is consensus
still wrong about tariffs? Our answer,
yes. At least that's what the August PBI
data signaled. While we always caution
against overreacting to any one data
point, this data point is consistent
with our long-held beliefs regarding the
outlook for US inflation. For our May
28th leadoff morning note, by late Q3,
early Q4, we believe Wall Street
consensus will have adopted our
structurally bullish paradigm C thesis
as their mode of outcome. The fact that
growth is slowing throughout is the
primary culprit for their delayed
recognition. Instead of positioning for
the continuation of the forth turning
inspired fiscal and monetary large s
plus substantial deregulation plus
restoring of critical industries each
being a durable positive shock to growth
many investors remain myopically focused
on the transitory negative shock to
growth from tariffs. Let Wall Street
consensus remain on the other side of
our trades while they continue to get
swindled by the mainstream media's
overselling of fear. fear that will no
doubt ratchet higher in the coming
months as resilient still resilient hard
data catches converges towards
unresilient soft data. Undisiplined
investors will overreact to the growth
slowdown and use it to support their
confirmation bias. Moreover,
congressional brinksmanship regarding
the one big beautiful bill act and
incremental brinksmanship regarding
trade negotiations are also likely to
trigger volatility this summer faded. We
continue to believe there is a high
probability that the next 20% delta in
the US stock market will be higher, not
lower. The next 10% delta may be lower,
but who cares? Buy the dip until you
officially until you hear us officially
dismiss paradigm C as the modal outcome.
And for those Cuban score at home, the
S&P 500 is up 11% since May 28th. Hope
you guys have been participating. An
alternative view uh regarding the impact
of tariffs upon the US economy is that
the Trump administration may be cooking
the inflation books. If the
administration is cooking the inflation
books, allegedly, don't get upset by it.
And more importantly, don't ever let
your emotions affect your portfolio.
Remember, your most important goal in
the current risk on market regime is to
maximize upside capture during our
raging Paradigm C bull market. Don't
worry about minimizing downside capture
until KISS and Dr. Mo signal that
paradigm C is over or about to take a
hazardous intermission. All told, our
general take on tariffs remains
unchanged from a month ago when the July
PBI data spooked investors via price
again a directionally hawkish Fed for
our August 16th around the horn.
Investors should expect the following
three things to occur over the next 12
to 18 months. Reported number one,
reported inflation is heading higher,
but generally not at a speed faster than
the consensus already anticipates.
Thursday's PBI beat notwithstanding.
Number two, the timing of tariff related
price increases will be nonlinear and
vary across sectors and companies based
on profit margin cushions. And number
three, at some point the tariff impulse
will wear off and inflation will start
heading lower again. The FOMC will be
foolish to overreact to these
proactively predictable transitory
dynamics with an illtime defense of
their arbitrary 2% inflation target.
Transition to our 42 micro dashboard. As
always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our uh community. Uh this one's
titled impact of employment provisions.

to the CBI data. I want to say those

and positioning cycles that's it and the

revisions confirm is that hey, Darius
Dell's right on his U-shaped economy
thesis, you know, when we authored the
paradigm C view, the the paradigm C view
uh contain within it, hey, we're
actually in a U-shaped economy, but the
whole point of paradigm C is that the
economy is going to U, not L or I, go
straight down. It's not going to L, go
down, and stay down. it's going to go
down, kiss the bottom, and start to
reacelerate again as a function of all
the dynamics that we carefully laid out
in the context of that paradigm C thesis
theme which we authored back in April.
Um, so uh, you know, I think it's just
more confirmation of the U-shaped
economy. So, um, and the more
confirmation we get of the U-shaped
economy, the more likely it is the
Federal Reserve, uh, undoes the pow
Fed's fifth major policy mistake by
supplying the monetary easing that's
priced in that the labor market very
clearly needs because it's screaming
help. according to the minus 911 911
revisions to the QECW report. Uh and

thought were very thoughtful as well. Uh
so I figured we uh let's unpack these as

think that the odds of that are are very
high. we would have seen it um in a

our uh 42 macro business cycle model to
give us a very uh what I think is one of
the industry's best, you know, kind of
forward-looking views on the probability
of uh of of of experiencing andor being
in a recession over a medium-term time
horizon, that network signal we were
likely to be in recession. Uh when you
look at initial and continuing jobs
claims potent particularly the
three-month annualized rate of change of
the fourth moving average of initial
claims and the three-month annualized
rate of change of continuing claims. Uh
we breached the recession and signaling
threshold uh five times in the initial
claims data over the past few years,
four times in the continuing claims data
over the past few years and each time
we've actually gone back down. And so
that's consistent with um our friend
Mike Wilson over at Morgan Stanley. He's
had this view for a few years now that
hey the economy is experiencing these
rolling recessions where they're you
know as opposed to one big broad uh
based decline in consumption production
income and investment which is what the
NBR is looking for when they put the red
bar the gray bar on the charts of
reviewing uh instead of experiencing
that we're experiencing hey this sector
has gone into recession and and and
eventually come out that sector followed
by this sector that industry this
industry that company this company and
ultimately uh part of this kind of
K-shaped economy thesis that we've been
espousing is that the the small
businesses and the low to median income
consumers have not recovered from their
rolling recessions. They're just like
it's just been a languishing multiple
years of crappy um uh economic uh um
outcomes for them uh uh for a variety of
reasons. One of the reasons is that the
Fed is not playing ball from a fiscal
monetary uh financial pressure and
monetary debasement perspective and
allowing enough capital formation uh uh
among those cohorts of the economy. And
so we think that's coming. Uh that's
part of the paradigm C thesis. It's not
going to come anytime soon or we're not
going to get enough of it on a very
short time horizon, but we know that
over a long-term time horizon. We
believe over a long-term time horizon
that it is coming and that's not
something you want to stand in the way
of as an investor in terms of trying to
short the market prematurely or not
maximizing upside capture in paradigm C.
And so, um, what's the impact of a
recession being declared going forward?
I mean, who cares if a recession was in

going to say on on True Social if that

just ringing out the the crap from the

it's going to have much of a market
impact. Uh aren't recessions usually
short? Uh I hate the word usually. Um
you know, the word usually implies like
we should be expecting um you know, some
sort of kind of like cookie cutter
outcome. Uh every business cycle is
different. Every market cycle is
different. you know, the the way the way
in which we view the world is through
the prism of regime segmentation. And
so, uh, we recognize that there is, you
know, dispersion within each regime, but
there's general things that we should
take away from each regime. Um, and so,
uh, you know, you know, recessions being
usually short or usually long, I don't
think it's going to really make a
difference here. Uh, if in fact the
economy was in a recession in 2024, the
first half of 2025, uh, you know, I
don't think that's going to have much of
a market impact. Uh and then finally has
the likelihood risen uh that uh 2026

show upper momentum first down the
regularly revised 2024 and 2025. To me
that's the biggest impact of all this
and I'm glad they actually put this in
the in the in the question. The worse
the economy was looking backwards the
better it's going to be looking forward
when you think and you know you when you
uh think about the growth rates uh the
year-over-year rates have changed the
quarter quarter rates have changed. Uh,
and so the worst economy was in the
first half of 2025, the better it'll
likely look in the second half of 2025.
Uh, the worse the economy was in 2024
and 2025, the better we'll likely look
in 2026. And this goes back to something
we've been talking about uh since we
altered the Paradigm C theme back in
April, which is consensus growth
estimates are way too low. We saw in the
the the biggest beat in um in quarterly
sales and earnings in the second quarter
since uh 2021, almost four years. uh uh
if you look at our shortrun potential
GDP growth expectation uh for the US
economy that's the blend of the current
year and the next year growth estimate
among Wall Street economist consensus uh
that gives us a a proxy for you know
what's the general level of growth that
you know uh you know strategists and and
analysts are kind of inputting into
their models to strip away the impact of
ACE effects that number went down from
2.2% a peak of 2.2% 2% in February to a
trough of 1.5% in April and has
meandered higher to 1.7% uh uh uh here
uh in September. 1.7% is too low in the
context of paradigm C. The economy could
grow 3 to 4% next year
could grow 3 to 4% next year on a real
basis. Uh if we're right on paradigm C
and so uh you know the consensus
estimates are for growth are way too
low. And this goes back to what we
started the question today in today's
macro. Is consensus still wrong about
tariffs? In our opinion, yes, we've been

essentially calling out saying, "Hey,

down way too low both on u from a macro

standpoint for earnings and they've uh

high uh in terms of uh the expectations
for CPI core PC and all we've gotten

performance being the more important

and the market performance are telling
consensus that it's wrong about tariffs.

if we're going to be honest, it's kind

was was wrong uh this spring and this
summer. And so hopefully everybody
